topic: Section 5.0 (ks-april-2026-update) — EXP-218 two-paragraph rewrite of 'Credit the criterion' and 'What the criterion does to this page'
our_argument_summary: EXP-218 replaces the two paragraphs that EXP-193 wrote inside the ks-april-2026-update <details> block. Paragraph 1 (Credit the criterion) now adds two qualifications to the unqualified credit it formerly gave: (i) Lakatos (1970) degenerative-research-program framing on rules tightened after embarrassing rows are demoted, and (ii) explicit naming of the asymmetry between the new (globe-only) discriminator standard and the deleted dome-falsifiability commitment. Paragraph 2 (What the criterion does to this page) replaces the survive-verb-ambiguous 'rows that survive are roughly the ones our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data' with a per-row three-prong walk through all six kill-shot rows, concluding that the new rule removes (or fails to rescue) all six.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: N/A — Section 5.0 is meta-commentary on a dated author edit (2026-04-17 commit 5021eec to docs/killshot.html), not a per-WIN calculation panel. EXP-218 does not touch the verbatim before/after rule table, the table reproducing the demoted row labels, the CSS-class mismatch paragraph, or the OTS companion-concession paragraph.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — no monitoring claims in this section.
data_sources_used: John09289/predictions GitHub repo for commit attribution
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — EXP-218 does not introduce code or monitoring claims.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section 5.0 is meta-commentary on an author edit; no dome monitor.py domain or live-fetch logic is implicated.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Not applicable — this section discusses the dome's own falsification rule rewrite, not a relabelling of standard physics.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 2 attributes the Polaris-row prong-3 failure to 'a 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction.' This contradicts our own Part 5 Test 2 detail text, which states refraction at 35° elevation contributes only ~0.02–0.03° — far smaller than the 0.27° claimed excess. Test 2's actual error-budget argument is dominated by Polaris's own polar offset (±0.66°) and field measurement uncertainty (±0.2–0.5°), with refraction explicitly listed as 'small but nonzero.' A dome defender can quote Test 2 against Section 5.0 to undermine the prong-3 attribution: 'Your own Test 2 says refraction is 0.02° — refraction cannot cover a 0.27° excess; the row should fail prong 2 (mechanically discriminating, because the signal is below detection threshold), not prong 3.' This is exactly the cross-WIN-consistency failure mode the c2 review's H1 (survive-verb ambiguity) was guarding against, just relocated.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'a 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction' with 'a 0.27° excess inside the field-measurement error budget — Polaris's own ±0.66° polar offset plus sextant uncertainty of ±0.2–0.5° per Test 2 — so the signal is below detection threshold and the row fails the "mechanically discriminating" prong.' This realigns the prong attribution with our Test 2 verdict basis (no statistically significant detection) and removes the refraction-coverage overstatement. Either prong-2 or prong-3 framing is defensible for Polaris, but the chosen prong must be supported by Test 2's actual argument, not a paraphrase Test 2 doesn't make.

holes_found.description: The 'removes all six' scope claim is slightly overdriven for the NMP row. Paragraph 2 walks NMP as 'self-reports 39.9% error on its own metric, so there is no "clean result" for the rule to apply to in the first place.' That is fair as far as it goes, but it changes the logic mid-walk: the other five rows are 'removed' because they fail a specific prong; NMP is 'removed' because the rule never applies. A defender can argue the new rule does NOT remove NMP — NMP simply isn't a candidate for the rule's protection because it has no clean result. The rule might still rescue NMP in the future if the error closes; the rule isn't doing the removal work — the 39.9% self-report is. The 'removes all six' header therefore conflates 'rule removes' with 'rule fails to rescue.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) soften the opening to 'demotes or fails to rescue all six' and keep the per-row walk as-is, or (b) keep 'removes all six' but explicitly distinguish in the NMP sentence: '...so the rule does not protect this row; the row is already refuted by its own self-report regardless of which framing is applied.' Option (a) is the smaller textual change.

holes_found.description: The Lakatos (1970) degenerative-research-program citation in paragraph 1 is functionally accurate but rhetorically vulnerable. Lakatos's paradigmatic degenerative move is RETAINING the original theory while adding ad hoc auxiliary hypotheses to neutralize counter-evidence; the dome author DEMOTED the embarrassing rows (a partial retraction) at the same time as rewriting the rule. A philosophy-of-science-literate defender can argue: 'Demotion is honest revision, not auxiliary protection. Lakatos's criterion isn't a clean fit when the author concedes the bad rows.' Our paragraph anticipates this only obliquely (qualification (i) names the same-commit coincidence but doesn't address the asymmetry between adding auxiliaries vs. retracting claims).
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the Lakatos sentence to make the specific degenerative pattern visible: 'fits part of the pattern Lakatos (1970) called a degenerative research programme — auxiliary criteria tightened in the same commit that retired exactly the rows the new criteria would have failed, with no new predictive content generated by the rewrite.' This narrows the claim to what is actually defensible (same-commit coincidence + no new predictions) and concedes the partial retraction without abandoning the frame.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 1's closing kicker — 'a tighter-on-globe and silent-on-dome rule is a narrower rule, not a strictly stronger one' — is rhetorically neat but slightly lossy. The new rule isn't actively anti-dome-falsifiability; it just doesn't restate the symmetric commitment the old rule carried. 'Silent-on-dome' implies a deliberate scope choice; the rewrite may simply have been about the discriminator threshold and not about dome-falsifiability at all. The asymmetry critique stands but the phrasing reads as harder than the textual evidence supports.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional softening: 'a rule that tightens what counts against the globe while no longer restating what counts against the dome is a narrower rule, not a strictly stronger one.' Lower-priority — the existing phrasing is defensible and the anticipated_objections section already pre-empts the obvious defender response.

holes_found.description: Both new paragraphs introduce additional bare <a href="#part5"> anchors (paragraph 2 cross-links to Tests 3–5 and Test 6 using only the page-level anchor). This re-instances the c1-flagged ISS-1216 problem inside the new content: a reader clicking 'Test 3–5' lands at the top of Part 5 and must scroll to find the relevant rows. The kill-shot test details blocks lack stable per-test ids in part5.html (only ks-april-2026-update has an id), so the bare anchor is the best currently available — but the new content increases the surface area where the navigation friction shows up.
holes_found.recommendation: Out of scope for a 2-paragraph rewrite. Flag for a separate structural EXP that adds per-test anchor ids (e.g., id="ks-test-3", "ks-test-6") to the test details blocks, then retrofits all #part5 cross-references to those targets. Track under existing ISS-1216.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 2 opens with 'once the four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched are walked across the three-prong test, it removes all six.' Syntactically this primes the reader to expect a walk of four rows; the body then walks all six (the two demoted rows AND the four untouched). The sentence reads as if the four untouched rows alone justify 'all six,' which they don't — the demoted rows are walked too. Local grammatical mismatch only; meaning is recoverable from the body.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional micro-edit: 'once each row — including the four the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched — is walked across the three-prong test, it removes all six.' Or just: '...applied honestly to all six rows, the new rule removes the kill-shot status from each.' Minor; safe to defer.

holes_found.description: STANDING from c1/c2 H7 (ISS-1218): TLDR phrase 'precisely the two retrospective/post-window rows on a six-row page' uses 'precisely' for a slightly imprecise classification — Polaris's window-status is debatable (the sextant measurement is attributed to 2025, but ongoing observations are possible). EXP-218 explicitly held scope to the two paragraphs and did not touch the TLDR, which is correct surgical discipline. Re-flagging only so the standing finding is not lost.
holes_found.recommendation: No action required for this EXP. Tracked under ISS-1218; analyst should propose a separate TLDR-only EXP that drops 'precisely' or replaces with 'the two retrospective rows.'

stronger_arguments.description: The asymmetry framing in paragraph 1 (qualification ii) could be strengthened by noting that the dome's site has a separate FAILURES tab listing dome-side falsifications (FAIL-001, etc.). The author has not abandoned dome-falsifiability as a site-wide concept — it just no longer appears on the kill-shot page's headline rule. This pre-empts the defender objection 'the dome-falsifiability commitment moved elsewhere' that the EXP-218 anticipated_objections section already addresses, by showing that we KNOW where the commitment lives (the FAILURES tab) and are specifically observing its disappearance from the kill-shot rule.
sources: FAIL-001 (Polaris-latitude internal contradiction noted in our own Test 2 detail)

stronger_arguments.description: The Lakatos qualification (i) could be paired with a positive contrast — Popper (1959, 'The Logic of Scientific Discovery') explicitly distinguishes 'conventional ad hoc' moves (which devalue the theory) from genuine theoretical revision (which preserves predictive content). The dome's row demotions ARE genuine revision (admitting the rows were oversold), but the rule rewrite added no new predictive content; that's the conventionalist twist Popper warned about. Adding a single Popper sentence makes the frame more philosophically careful and reduces the 'name-dropping' anticipated objection.
sources: Popper, K. (1959). The Logic of Scientific Discovery, sections 19–20 on conventionalist twist

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-218 is a competent execution of the c2 review. All seven targeted holes (H1–H6 + H8) have textual closure: 'survive' verb is gone, asymmetry is named, per-row walk is added, interpretive reframing is signalled as ours, Lakatos citation is integrated, the methodological-appeal phrasing is replaced. The paragraph word count grew from 275 to ~470 words across both paragraphs — a reasonable expansion that keeps each paragraph under 250 words individually. The TLDR was correctly held out of scope per H7, and the surrounding section (verbatim before/after rule table, demoted-row table, CSS-mismatch paragraph, OTS companion-concession paragraph) is unchanged. This is exactly how a paragraph-level surgical patch should look.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The execution successfully closes the c2 holes but introduces a new major exposure (Polaris attribution to refraction contradicting our own Test 2) and inherits two moderate ones (NMP scope conflation, Lakatos paradigmatic-fit gap). The structural improvement is preserved; the new surface text needs a one-sentence Polaris fix (and ideally the NMP-scope and Lakatos-tightening tweaks) before the section is bulletproof against a careful defender.

text_fingerprint.verdict: meta-section, no per-row verdict — credits self-correction while preserving dated record; Lakatos and asymmetry framings now integrated

deeper_analysis: The two new paragraphs do the work the c2 review asked for. The chief residual exposure is the Polaris row's prong-3 attribution to atmospheric refraction. Our own Test 2 quantifies refraction at 35° elevation as ~0.02–0.03° — an order of magnitude smaller than the 0.27° claimed excess. The dominant cover for the Polaris excess in Test 2 is Polaris's polar offset (±0.66°) plus sextant noise (±0.2–0.5°), with refraction listed as a minor contribution. By singling out refraction as the prong-3 'competing explanation' in Section 5.0, paragraph 2 hands a defender a clean cross-section quote: 'You said refraction. Your own Test 2 says refraction is 0.02°. Refraction cannot cover 0.27°.' The fix is one sentence — either reattribute Polaris to prong 2 (mechanically discriminating: signal below detection threshold) or to prong 3 with the correct cover (polar offset + measurement uncertainty, not refraction). Beyond Polaris, the paragraph-level scope discipline is good — TLDR, before/after table, CSS-mismatch paragraph, and OTS companion-concession paragraph were all correctly held out of scope. The Lakatos citation is functionally correct but invites a paradigmatic-fit nitpick; the 'tighter-on-globe and silent-on-dome' kicker is rhetorically punchy but not strictly necessary. Net: the section is materially stronger than after EXP-193, and the residual fixes are surgical (one major one-sentence fix, two moderate softenings, a couple of minor stylistic tweaks).

citations_verified: Lakatos (1970), 'Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes' — verified as the canonical title of the essay published in Lakatos & Musgrave (eds.) Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge (Cambridge UP, 1970). Degenerative-vs-progressive research programme distinction is the central concept.
Commit 5021eec on John09289/predictions @ 2026-04-17 07:52 EDT — verified in the unchanged surrounding paragraphs of the same section (verbatim quote and timestamp present).
Commit 6e06efb (OTS canary companion concession) — referenced in the unchanged OTS companion-concession paragraph; not touched by EXP-218.
citations_failed: 'a 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction' — INTERNAL citation failure. Our own Part 5 Test 2 detail text states 'Atmospheric refraction at 35° elevation adds ~0.02–0.03° (small but nonzero)' and assigns dominant cover to Polaris's polar offset (±0.66°) and field measurement uncertainty (±0.2–0.5°). The Section 5.0 paragraph 2 attribution to refraction is inconsistent with Test 2's own quantification. Treat as a hole_found H1 (major) and not as an external citation failure.
citations_unchecked: Lakatos's full essay text (1970) — title and concept are standard references; a full re-read of the essay was not performed for this review.
